title: APK
description: An **APK (Android Package Kit)** is the binary distribution format used to install and run Android apps on a device or emulator. It is a ZIP archive containing compiled bytecode (DEX files), resources, assets, and the `AndroidManifest.xml`. While APKs are still used for sideloading and local testing, Google Play now prefers the AAB format for production distribution.

resources.title: Application fundamentals | Android Developers
APK vs AAB: Understanding Android app packaging formats
What Is an APK File on Android? | YouTube
Android Developer Roadmap | roadmap.sh

title: AAB
description: An **Android App Bundle (AAB)** is the recommended publishing format for Google Play. Unlike an APK, an AAB defers APK generation and signing to Google Play, which uses **Play Feature Delivery** to serve optimized APKs tailored to each device's screen density, ABI, and language — reducing download sizes significantly. AAB is required for new apps published to Google Play since August 2021.

resources.title: Android App Bundle overview | Android Developers
Building your first app bundle | Android Developers Blog
App Bundles: Testing bundles with bundletool and the Play Console - MAD Skills | Android Developers
google/bundletool — Command-line tool to manipulate Android App Bundles | GitHub

title: Kotlin
description: **Kotlin** is the primary and officially recommended language for Android development, offering concise syntax, null safety, coroutines for async programming, and full interoperability with Java. Google has been **Kotlin-first** since 2019, meaning new Jetpack APIs and documentation examples prioritize Kotlin. Its features — extension functions, data classes, sealed classes — map naturally to common Android patterns.

resources.title: Kotlin on Android | Android Developers
Kotlin language reference | kotlinlang.org
Kotlin for Android: An Introduction | ProAndroidDev
Kotlin Crash Course | Philipp Lackner
Kotlin Developer Roadmap | roadmap.sh
Developing Android Apps with Kotlin | Udacity (free, by Google)

title: K2 Compiler
description: The **K2 Compiler** is Kotlin's rewritten frontend compiler, stabilized in Kotlin 2.0, delivering up to **2x faster compilation** compared to the classic compiler. It provides a unified compiler pipeline for all Kotlin targets (JVM, JS, Native, Wasm) and improves smart-cast analysis and type inference accuracy. Android projects using AGP 8.3+ can enable K2 via `kotlin.version=2.0.x` in `gradle.properties`.

resources.title: K2 compiler | Kotlin 2.0 | kotlinlang.org
What's new in Kotlin 2.0 and K2 compiler | Android Developers Blog
Migrating An Android Project To K2 Compiler | YouTube
K2 Compiler migration guide | Kotlin Documentation

title: Java (legacy)
description: Java was the original language for Android development and remains fully supported via the Android SDK and toolchain. However, **Kotlin is now the recommended language** for all new Android projects, offering a more concise and safer alternative. Existing Java codebases can interoperate with Kotlin seamlessly, making incremental migration feasible. Java on Android targets the Java 8 language subset with some Java 11 APIs available via desugaring.

resources.title: Java 8+ APIs available through desugaring | Android Developers
Migrating from Java to Kotlin in Android | ProAndroidDev
Android Development for Beginners - Full Course (Java) | freeCodeCamp
Java Developer Roadmap | roadmap.sh

title: C++ (JNI)
description: The **Java Native Interface (JNI)** enables Android apps to call native C or C++ code compiled via the **NDK (Native Development Kit)**. This is used for performance-critical work (codecs, signal processing, physics engines), reusing existing native libraries, or accessing low-level hardware APIs not exposed to the SDK. Use JNI sparingly — it increases complexity and bypasses Android's managed memory model.

resources.title: Get started with the NDK | Android Developers
JNI tips | Android Developers
Android NDK: Getting started with C++ in Android | ProAndroidDev
android/ndk-samples — Android NDK samples with Android Studio | GitHub
C++ Developer Roadmap | roadmap.sh

title: Multi-User Linux
description: Android is built on Linux and uses its **multi-user security model** to isolate apps from each other. Each installed application is assigned a unique Linux user ID (UID), and the OS enforces that an app's files and processes are inaccessible to other apps by default. This architecture means the security guarantees of Linux's user model underpin the entire Android security model.

resources.title: Application fundamentals — security sandbox | Android Developers
Android Security: Understanding the Linux security model | ProAndroidDev
Security for Android Developers | Android Developers YouTube
Application Sandbox | Android Open Source Project

title: File Permissions
description: Android inherits Linux **discretionary access control (DAC)** for file permissions, using standard `read`, `write`, and `execute` bits tied to each app's UID and GID. App-private files stored under `/data/data/<package>` are owned by the app's UID and are unreadable by other apps. Understanding file permissions is essential for securely storing sensitive data and correctly configuring `FileProvider` when sharing files between apps.

resources.title: Data and file storage overview | Android Developers
Android File Permissions and Storage | ProAndroidDev
Security for Android Developers | Android Developers YouTube
System and kernel security | Android Open Source Project

title: Resource Isolations
description: Android enforces **resource isolation** so that each app operates within its own security sandbox — with its own process, heap, and file space. Apps cannot directly access memory, CPU time, or files belonging to other apps without explicit IPC mechanisms. This isolation also extends to storage: app-specific directories are inaccessible to other apps, and shared storage is governed by scoped storage rules on Android 10+.

resources.title: Android security overview | Android Developers
Android Security: Application Sandbox | ProAndroidDev
Security for Android Developers | Android Developers YouTube
Application Sandbox | Android Open Source Project

title: Process Management
description: Each Android app runs in its own **Linux process** with a dedicated Dalvik/ART virtual machine instance, started when a component is launched. The Android system uses a **process importance hierarchy** (foreground, visible, service, cached) to determine which processes to kill when memory is low. Developers should understand this hierarchy to ensure critical work (e.g., in foreground services) survives under memory pressure.

resources.title: Processes and app lifecycle | Android Developers
Android process lifecycle and app death | ProAndroidDev
Explained — Android UI thread / main thread | YouTube
Processes and threads overview | Android Developers

title: Sandboxing & SELinux
description: Android uses **SELinux (Security-Enhanced Linux)** in enforcing mode to implement mandatory access control (MAC) on top of the standard Linux DAC model. SELinux policies define which processes can access which files, sockets, and system calls — even if a process runs as root. This defense-in-depth approach contains the damage from a compromised process and is a cornerstone of Android's platform security.

resources.title: Security-Enhanced Linux in Android | Android Developers
Android Security: SELinux and MAC explained | ProAndroidDev
Security for Android Developers | Android Developers YouTube
SELinux concepts | Android Open Source Project

title: Scoped Storage
description: **Scoped Storage**, introduced in Android 10 and enforced in Android 11, restricts apps to their own app-specific directories and well-defined collections (MediaStore, Downloads) without requiring broad storage permissions. Apps must use the **MediaStore API** or the **Storage Access Framework** to access files outside their sandbox. This model protects user privacy by preventing apps from freely browsing the file system.

resources.title: Storage updates in Android 11 | Android Developers
Data and file storage overview | Android Developers
Scoped Storage in Android 11: Everything you need to know | ProAndroidDev
Preparing for Scoped Storage (Android Dev Summit '19) | Android Developers

title: The Linux Kernel
description: The **Linux Kernel** forms the foundation of Android's platform architecture, providing core OS services: process management, memory management, networking, and a hardware driver model. Android uses a modified Linux kernel with additional features such as Binder IPC, ashmem (anonymous shared memory), and the Low Memory Killer. All higher Android layers ultimately depend on kernel services for hardware access and process isolation.

resources.title: Android platform architecture | Android Developers
Understanding Android Architecture: Linux Kernel layer | ProAndroidDev
Security for Android Developers | Android Developers YouTube
System and kernel security | Android Open Source Project

title: Hardware Abstraction Layer
description: The **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)** defines standard interfaces that hardware vendors implement, allowing the Android framework to call hardware functionality without knowing driver specifics. HALs are packaged as shared libraries (`.so` files) and are loaded at runtime. Modern Android uses **HIDL** (HAL Interface Definition Language) and the newer **AIDL**-based HALs to version these interfaces and enable independent updates via Project Treble.

resources.title: Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL) | Android Open Source Project
Android HAL: How hardware abstraction works | ProAndroidDev
Android's HIDL: Treble in the HAL | YouTube
Architecture overview | Android Open Source Project

title: Android Runtime (ART)
description: **ART (Android Runtime)** is the managed runtime that executes compiled Android apps, replacing the older Dalvik runtime since Android 5.0. ART uses **Ahead-Of-Time (AOT) compilation**, **Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation**, and **profile-guided optimization** to deliver fast execution and reduced startup times. It also provides garbage collection, debugging support, and profiling infrastructure used throughout the Android ecosystem.

resources.title: Android Runtime (ART) and Dalvik | Android Open Source Project
ART and Dalvik: Android runtime explained | ProAndroidDev
Understanding Android Runtime (ART) for faster apps (Google I/O '19) | Android Developers

title: Native Libraries
description: Android's **Native Libraries** layer consists of C/C++ libraries that provide core functionality to the framework, including **OpenGL ES** (graphics), **SQLite** (database), **WebKit** (web rendering), **libc**, and SSL. These libraries are exposed to apps via the Java API Framework or directly via the NDK. Understanding which native capabilities are available helps when choosing between SDK-level APIs and lower-level native access.

resources.title: Android platform architecture — Native C/C++ Libraries | Android Developers
Android Native Libraries Overview | ProAndroidDev
How to create Driver & HAL in Android (AOSP)? | YouTube
android/ndk-samples — Android NDK samples with Android Studio | GitHub

title: Java API Framework
description: The **Java API Framework** is the set of Android APIs written in Java/Kotlin that app developers use daily — including the Activity Manager, Window Manager, Content Providers, View System, Package Manager, and Notification Manager. It sits atop the native libraries and ART runtime, exposing their capabilities through stable, versioned APIs. This layer is what developers interact with through `android.*` and `androidx.*` packages.

resources.title: Android platform architecture — Java API Framework | Android Developers
Android Architecture: Java API Framework explained | ProAndroidDev
Understanding Android Runtime (ART) for faster apps (Google I/O '19) | Android Developers
Android Basics with Compose | Free course by Google

title: System Apps
description: **System Apps** are pre-installed applications (Dialer, SMS, Camera, Settings, Browser) that ship with Android and have elevated permissions not available to third-party apps. They occupy the topmost layer of the Android platform architecture and serve as default handlers for common actions (e.g., `ACTION_DIAL`). Third-party apps can replace some system apps as default handlers, but cannot gain system-level permissions without signing with the platform key.

resources.title: Android platform architecture — System Apps | Android Developers
Android System Apps vs Regular Apps: Differences explained | ProAndroidDev
Security for Android Developers | Android Developers YouTube
aosp-mirror/platform_frameworks_base — AOSP frameworks base mirror | GitHub

title: AICore
description: **AICore** is an Android system service (introduced with Pixel 8 / Android 14) that manages on-device GenAI foundation model execution, including Gemini Nano. It handles model distribution via Play, hardware-accelerated inference, and safety features — all while adhering to **Private Compute Core** principles so data never leaves the device. Developers access AICore capabilities through the **ML Kit GenAI APIs** for tasks like summarization, proofreading, and prompt generation.

resources.title: Gemini Nano on Android (AICore) | Android Developers
Announcing Gemma 4 in the AICore Developer Preview | Android Developers Blog
Gemini Nano on Android: Building with on-device gen AI | Android Developers
ML Kit GenAI APIs overview | Google for Developers

title: Package
description: The `package` attribute in `AndroidManifest.xml` historically served as both the app's unique identifier and the namespace for generated R and BuildConfig classes. Since AGP 7.3+, the **namespace** (in `build.gradle`) handles the code namespace, while `applicationId` in `build.gradle` defines the Play Store identifier. The manifest `package` attribute is still read at build time but is effectively superseded by these two distinct Gradle properties.

resources.title: App manifest overview | Android Developers
Package name vs Application ID in Android | ProAndroidDev
Android Basics with Compose — Introduction to Kotlin | Android Developers
Android Basics with Compose | Free course by Google

title: Application ID
description: The **Application ID** (`applicationId` in `build.gradle`) is the globally unique identifier for your app on the device and on Google Play, following reverse-domain notation (e.g., `com.example.myapp`). It is set independently of the code namespace and cannot be changed after publishing without breaking existing installs and deep links. Build variants can use `applicationIdSuffix` to produce distinct IDs for debug/staging builds.

resources.title: Set the application ID | Android Studio | Android Developers
Understanding Application ID in Android | ProAndroidDev
App Bundles: Testing bundles with bundletool and the Play Console - MAD Skills | Android Developers
Android Basics with Compose | Free course by Google

title: Permissions Declaration
description: Apps must declare every permission they need in `AndroidManifest.xml` using `<uses-permission>`. **Dangerous permissions** (camera, location, contacts) additionally require runtime requests via `ActivityResultContracts.RequestPermission` since Android 6.0. The system enforces declared permissions at install time (normal) or at runtime (dangerous), and undeclared permissions are silently denied — making thorough manifest declarations essential for functionality and store compliance.

resources.title: Permissions on Android | Android Developers
Android Runtime Permissions: A Guide | ProAndroidDev
How to Request Runtime Permissions in Android | YouTube

title: Foreground Service Types
description: Since **Android 14**, apps targeting API 34+ must declare a `android:foregroundServiceType` attribute on each `<service>` element in the manifest, or calling `startForeground()` will throw `MissingForegroundServiceTypeException`. Available types include `camera`, `location`, `mediaPlayback`, `dataSync`, `health`, `microphone`, and others. Specifying the correct type also unlocks corresponding runtime permissions (e.g., `FOREGROUND_SERVICE_CAMERA`) required to start that service type.

resources.title: Foreground service types are required | Android 14 | Android Developers
Foreground Service Types in Android 14: What changed | ProAndroidDev
Foreground Service in Android 14 | YouTube
Foreground service types reference | Android Developers

title: Queries / Package Visibility
description: Starting with **Android 11 (API 30)**, apps targeting API 30+ can no longer see all installed packages by default — protecting user privacy. To interact with another app, your app must declare the target package name, intent filter signature, or content provider authority in a `<queries>` element in the manifest. This affects any code using `queryIntentActivities()`, `getPackageInfo()`, or `Intent.resolveActivity()` to discover other apps.

resources.title: Package visibility filtering on Android | Android Developers
<queries> manifest element | Android Developers
Android 11 Package Visibility: Migrating your app | ProAndroidDev
Package visibility in Android 11 | Android Developers Medium

title: Activity
description: An **Activity** is the primary UI entry point of an Android app, representing a single screen with a user interface. Activities manage their own lifecycle (`onCreate` → `onStart` → `onResume` → `onPause` → `onStop` → `onDestroy`) and must be declared in the manifest. Modern Android development favors a **single-Activity** architecture using Fragments or Compose navigation destinations, reducing the complexity of managing multiple Activity lifecycles.

resources.title: Introduction to activities | Android Developers
Single Activity: Why, When, and How | Android Developers Blog
Activities & the Activity Lifecycle - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner
Android Basics with Compose | Free course by Google

title: Service
description: A **Service** is an app component that performs long-running operations in the background without a UI, such as music playback, file downloads, or network sync. Services come in two main forms: **started services** (run until stopped) and **bound services** (run while clients are connected). Since Android 8.0, background service execution is restricted — use **WorkManager** for deferrable background work and **Foreground Services** for user-visible ongoing tasks.

resources.title: Services overview | Android Developers
Android Services: Everything you need to know | ProAndroidDev
Foreground Services - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner
WorkManager - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Broadcast Receiver
description: A **Broadcast Receiver** allows an app to respond to system-wide or app-specific broadcast messages (e.g., network changes, battery low, custom app events). Receivers can be registered **statically** in the manifest (limited by background restrictions since Android 8.0) or **dynamically** in code via `registerReceiver()`. For ordered or local in-process communication, prefer `LocalBroadcastManager` (deprecated) or a reactive approach using **Flow** or **LiveData**.

resources.title: Broadcasts overview | Android Developers
Android BroadcastReceiver: A practical guide | ProAndroidDev
Broadcasts & Broadcast Receivers - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Content Provider
description: A **Content Provider** manages access to a structured set of data and exposes it to other apps via a URI-based interface, backed by SQLite, files, or any data source. It is the standard IPC mechanism for sharing data across app boundaries (e.g., Contacts, MediaStore). **FileProvider** (a subclass) is commonly used to share files between apps safely. Modern apps prefer the Room database for internal storage and only implement a Content Provider when cross-app data sharing is explicitly needed.

resources.title: Content providers | Android Developers
Android Content Providers: A complete guide | ProAndroidDev
Content Providers - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Intent Filters
description: **Intent Filters** declared in the manifest specify the types of implicit intents a component can handle, defined by action, category, and data URI scheme. The Android system uses intent filters to resolve which app (or component) should handle an incoming implicit intent, such as `ACTION_VIEW` for a URL or `ACTION_SEND` for sharing. Incorrect or overly broad intent filters can expose components to unintended callers — always validate incoming intent data.

resources.title: Intents and intent filters | Android Developers
Android Intent Filters: A deep dive | ProAndroidDev
Intents & Intent Filters - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Explicit Intents
description: An **Explicit Intent** directly names the target component (by class name) that should handle the intent, making it unambiguous. They are used for intra-app navigation (e.g., launching a specific Activity or Service within your own app) and are the preferred approach when the target is known. Explicit intents bypass the system's intent resolution process, making them faster and more secure than implicit intents for internal communication.

resources.title: Intents and intent filters — Explicit intents | Android Developers
Explicit vs Implicit Intents in Android | ProAndroidDev
Intents & Intent Filters - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Implicit Intents
description: An **Implicit Intent** declares a general action to perform (e.g., `ACTION_VIEW`, `ACTION_SEND`) without naming a specific component, letting the system find the best matching app via intent filters. If multiple apps can handle the intent, the user is presented with a chooser. Since Android 11, you must declare the target package in `<queries>` to resolve implicit intents to specific third-party apps — and always call `resolveActivity()` to check that a handler exists before launching.

resources.title: Intents and intent filters — Implicit intents | Android Developers
Implicit Intents and Android 11 Package Visibility | ProAndroidDev
Intents & Intent Filters - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: PendingIntent (mutability)
description: A **PendingIntent** wraps an Intent and grants another app or the system the ability to fire it on your app's behalf at a later time (used with notifications, alarms, and widgets). Since **Android 12 (API 31)**, apps must explicitly declare each PendingIntent as `FLAG_IMMUTABLE` or `FLAG_MUTABLE` — omitting the flag causes a crash. Prefer `FLAG_IMMUTABLE` unless the PendingIntent must be modified by the receiving app (e.g., inline reply actions).

resources.title: PendingIntent mutability | Android 12 | Android Developers
PendingIntent in Android 12: Mutability changes explained | ProAndroidDev
Intents & Intent Filters - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Activity Lifecycles
description: The **Activity lifecycle** defines the sequence of states an Activity moves through from creation to destruction: `onCreate()`, `onStart()`, `onResume()`, `onPause()`, `onStop()`, and `onDestroy()`. Each callback is the right place for specific operations — initialize UI in `onCreate`, release heavy resources in `onStop`. Using **LifecycleObserver** and **Jetpack ViewModel** helps avoid placing lifecycle logic directly in the Activity, keeping the class lean.

resources.title: The activity lifecycle | Android Developers
Understanding the Android Activity Lifecycle | ProAndroidDev
Activities & the Activity Lifecycle - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Activity State Changes
description: **Activity state changes** occur in response to user actions (rotating the device, pressing Back, switching apps) or system events (low memory kills, multi-window resizing). Configuration changes like rotation destroy and recreate the Activity by default; use **ViewModel** to survive data across this cycle and `onSaveInstanceState()` for lightweight transient UI state. Understanding when and why the system triggers each lifecycle transition is critical for preventing data loss and UI glitches.

resources.title: Activity state changes | Android Developers
Handling Activity State Changes in Android | ProAndroidDev
ViewModels & Configuration Changes - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Task and Back Stack
description: A **task** is a collection of activities arranged in a LIFO **back stack**, representing a user's flow through the app. When the user navigates forward, new activities are pushed; pressing Back pops them. Launch modes (`standard`, `singleTop`, `singleTask`, `singleInstance`) and `Intent` flags (`FLAG_ACTIVITY_NEW_TASK`, `FLAG_ACTIVITY_CLEAR_TOP`) control how activities are added to or reused within tasks. Understanding this model is essential for correct deep linking, notification navigation, and multi-app flows.

resources.title: Tasks and the back stack | Android Developers
Android Tasks and Back Stack: A complete guide | ProAndroidDev
Tasks, Back Stack & Launch Modes - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner

title: Parcelables and Bundles
description: **Parcelable** is Android's high-performance serialization interface for passing objects between components via `Intent` extras or `Bundle`. Unlike Java's `Serializable`, `Parcelable` is designed for inter-process communication and avoids reflection overhead. **Bundles** are key-value containers that hold Parcelable, primitive, and String data. Kotlin's `@Parcelize` annotation (from `kotlin-parcelize`) auto-generates boilerplate `Parcelable` implementations, making this pattern concise in modern code.

resources.title: Parcelables and Bundles | Android Developers
Parcelable vs Serializable in Android | ProAndroidDev
Parcelable in Kotlin — We had @Parcelize | YouTube
Parcelable implementation generator (@Parcelize) | Android Developers

title: App Shortcuts
description: **App Shortcuts** let users perform common actions in your app directly from the launcher (via long-press) or from Google Assistant. There are three types: **static** (defined in XML, packaged with the app), **dynamic** (pushed at runtime via `ShortcutManager`), and **pinned** (added to the home screen by the user). Shortcuts should represent high-value, frequently used actions and must be kept up to date as the app's state changes.

resources.title: App shortcuts overview | Android Developers
Android App Shortcuts: Static, Dynamic, and Pinned | ProAndroidDev
Activities & the Activity Lifecycle - Android Basics 2023 | Philipp Lackner
android/platform-samples — Android OS platform API samples | GitHub

title: Splash Screen API
description: The **Splash Screen API** (introduced in Android 12, backported via `androidx.core:core-splashscreen`) provides a standardized app launch animation showing the app icon on a solid background. It replaces the old workaround of using a theme with `windowBackground`. Developers can customize the icon, background color, and exit animation, and optionally delay dismissal using `ViewTreeObserver.OnPreDrawListener` until initial data is loaded.

resources.title: Splash screens | Android Developers
Implementing the Android 12 Splash Screen API | ProAndroidDev
Easily Implement Splash Screen in Android Using the NEW API! | YouTube

title: Activity Result API
description: The **Activity Result API** (`registerForActivityResult()` + `ActivityResultContract`) is the modern replacement for the deprecated `startActivityForResult()` / `onActivityResult()` pattern. It provides type-safe, lifecycle-aware result handling and ships with built-in contracts for common tasks (take photo, pick contact, request permission). Available via `androidx.activity` and `androidx.fragment`, it works seamlessly with both Fragments and Compose.

resources.title: Get a result from an activity | Android Developers
The Activity Result API: A deep dive | ProAndroidDev
Easily Pass Data Between Activities Using the NEW Activity Result API! | YouTube

title: Predictive Back Gesture
description: The **Predictive Back Gesture** (introduced in Android 13, fully enforced in Android 14+) shows users a preview of where the Back gesture will take them before they complete the swipe — improving navigational clarity. Apps must opt in by setting `android:enableOnBackInvokedCallback="true"` in the manifest and migrating away from the deprecated `onBackPressed()` to `OnBackPressedCallback` (AndroidX) or `OnBackInvokedCallback` (platform API). Compose apps use the `BackHandler` or `PredictiveBackHandler` composable.

resources.title: Add support for the predictive back gesture | Android Developers
Predictive Back Gesture in Android 13 and 14 | ProAndroidDev
How to Migrate Your App to Support the NEW Predictive Back Gestures On Android | YouTube
